coredata.dc:description: The present study provides a comprehensive overview of the effectiveness of nanofluids (NFs) in enhancing heat pipe (HP) performance, a critical component in thermal management applications such as electronics cooling, solar power conversion, and automotive systems. Traditional heat transfer fluids suffer from low thermal conductivity and heat transfer coefficients, causing a clear reduction of thermal efficacy. This review specifically addresses these limitations of traditional heat transfer fluids used in heat pipes by analysing both experimental and computational research from the open literature. Findings indicate that a the combination of nanoparticles, such as Al2O3, CuO, and carbon nanotubes can substantially enhance the thermal conductivity, mitigate thermal interface resistance, and boost the heat transfer coefficients. Additionally, factors such as nanoparticle concentration, particle size, and heat pipe geometry significantly influence overall thermal performance. Specifically, optimum nanoparticle concentrations (0.1–0.3 wt.%) are vital, as excessive loading can deter performance. Also, the performance fluctuates across various HP types, with geometric modifications further improving thermal efficiency. Even though short-term advantages are apparent, long-term stability concerns, such as nanoparticle agglomeration, necessitate future research. This study demonstrates the potential of nanofluids in heat pipes to advance next-generation thermal management technologies across different applications.

row.entry: The evaporator-condenser temperature differential was significantly reduced by the volume concentration of nanoparticles.

At low heat flow, the thermal resistance drops; at constant or slightly higher heat flux, it rises significantly. When compared to water, the heat resistance of functionalized nanofluid is 22–25 % greater.
The use of nanoparticles improves the heat pipe's TP and decreases the temperature distribution.
An excellent way to improve the heat pipe's thermal efficiency is to employ nanofluids as the working fluid; doing so considerably raises the heat pipe's heat transfer coefficient.

As the concentration of nanoparticles and the intensity of the electrical field grow, the thermal efficiency of the two-phase closed Thermo-syphon also increases. Furthermore, it demonstrates that compared to CuO nanoparticles, Al2O3 nanoparticles significantly improve thermal efficiency.

Increasing the input power and GNP concentration both lead to an increase in the total heat transfer coefficient. Covalent samples of GNP-COOH exhibit the highest increases in the heat transfer coefficient among the data.
At a weight percentage of 0.002 wt.%, A-GQD nanofluids dissolved in water improved the total heat transfer coefficient the most.
With the nanofluid present, the heat pipe's overall thermal resistance drops to its lowest point when the evaporator is subjected to the maximum heat flow.
A dramatic rise in the heat transfer coefficient is seen by the evaporation as a function of the mass concentrations.
When employing the nanofluid, the Thermo-syphon could operate at greater input powers (up to 90 W) than when using water (up to 70 W).

By substituting CuO/water nanofluids for pure water in TPCT, the performance improvement was shown. Nanofluids containing 1 % CuO/water and 2 % CuO/water showed an improvement of around 10 % and 18.5 %, respectively.

A Thermo-syphon using nanofluids might improve TP by lowering the evaporator temperature; this would result in a 41 %, 32 %, and 29 % reduction in total entropy production for Cu, Fe2O3, and Al2O3, respectively, as compared to pure water.
The thermal resistance rose as a result of a 58.3 % drop in surface tension and a 5.6 % drop in thermal conductivity.
TP was enhanced when 0.8 % (by volume) of Al2O3 Nanoparticles were added to water, as compared to running the operation with only water.

Al2O3: Cu: TiO2 ternary
hybrid nanofluids.

Nanofluids at a concentration of 0.3 vol.% under an 80 W heat load and a 90° inclination angle demonstrated the lowest thermal resistance of 0.09 K/W and the highest heat transfer enhancement efficiency of 22.5 %.

PVP-coated Nanoplates can attain maximum thermal conductivity, enhancing the heat transfer coefficient to 362 W/m² K at a 90 % filling ratio and 90° inclination, in a comparison to 350 W/m² K for distilled water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A summary of studies that are related to the NF use in the OHP.

row.entry: The OHP's heat transmission was improved by substituting alumina nanofluid for pure water. OHP TP was negatively affected when silica nanofluid was used instead of pure water.
At any input power to the evaporator, the aqueous nanofluids containing 0.2 weight percent MWCNTs display the lowest thermal resistance and the highest number of fluctuation frequencies.
In the evaporator portion, the temperature differential between the surface and the vapour core was 3.1°C for the Fe2O3 without a magnetic field and 2°C with one.
The self-rewetting nanofluid has a peak enhancement ratio of about 16 %, which is much higher than both the self-rewetting fluid and the nanofluid.
For an OHP filled with 2.0 vol.% GNP nanofluid, the highest decrease in thermal resistance was 83.6 % when compared to the DW OHP at the same filling ratio (62 %). The heating power was 80 W.
When the OHP is filled with 3.0 wt.% MWCNT nanofluid, an exceptionally high thermal conductivity of 6,000 W/m K is achieved.
With a 0.2 wt.% CNT concentration, the OHP's average evaporator temperature dropped to 43.1°C and its thermal resistance dropped to 0.066°C/W.
As the radius of the simulated NF made of Fe3O4-H2O increased, the HT/MT exhibited better thermal behavior.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The summary of studies that are related to the NF use in screen mesh HP.

row.entry: The evaporator-adiabatic segment of the heat pipe experiences a considerable 40 % reduction in thermal resistance when utilising water-based Al2O3 nanofluids with a volume fraction of 3.0 vol.%.
The heat pipe that is vertically installed and contains 0.5 weight percent Cu-distilled water nanofluid has a thermal resistance that is about 27 % lower.

At a concentration of 0.009 % vol.%, silver nanoparticles significantly lower heat resistance by 76.2 %. At the same concentration, there is a 52.7 % improvement in the evaporation heat transfer coefficient. Using nanoparticles increases the heat pipe's operational range as compared to DI water by 21 %.
Under identical operating circumstances, the heat pipe charged with 0.1 wt.% Cu-water nanofluid exhibited 20 % greater liquid and vapour velocities than the heat pipe charged with DI water.
At the same heat input, mass concentration, and inclination angle, sintered wick heat pipe experiences a 13.92 % greater decrease in thermal resistance than mesh wick heat pipe.
As the concentration of nanoparticles increased, the heat pipes' thermal performance improved relative to water, and the heat pipes' thermal resistance reduced.

When heat fluxes are increased, heat pipes containing 5 wt.% Al2O3 NF reduce thermal resistance by 6 % to 24 % and decrease it by 20 % to 55 %. In contrast, heat pipes containing 10 wt.% Al2O3 NF show an increase in thermal resistance of 187 % to 206 % for increases and a decrease of 155 % to 175 % for decreases.

The creation of a porous coating on the surface of the wick enhances its wettability and surface roughness, leading to the lowering of heat resistance. This, in turn, creates additional nucleation sites, as can be shown in the SEM pictures.
The optimal performance is seen when φ=5 % and Q=112 W.
Practical heat exchange applications may benefit from heat pipes that include Al2O3 nanoparticles suspended in the base fluid, which boosts their thermal efficiency.
There was greater variation in the capillary limit improvement and higher heat resistance at high nanoparticle concentrations compared to the working fluid without nanoparticles.
When compared to using distilled water as the working fluid, the heat transfer rate is higher when CuO nanofluids are loaded into regular water.
Theoretically, the heat resistance in the wick may be amplified by the extensive deposition of nanoparticles at concentrations greater than that ideal one.
Improved HTP was the outcome of a 1 vol.% concentration of Ag nanoparticles being added to the heat pipe, which decreased its thermal resistance.
In the evaporator part, the MgO-Al2O3 hybrid nanofluid improves surface wettability, which lowers thermal resistance.
The ideal temperature input, angle of inclination, and nanofluid concentration were found to be 200 W, 52.72°, and 0.1773 vol. %, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A summary of studies that are related to the NF use in WHP.

row.entry: When utilising Cu-water nanofluid with a volume percentage of 0.04 and nanoparticles with a radius of 5 nm, the maximum heat transfer by RHP at ΔT=20 °C and ω=3000 rpm rises by about 56 %.

By incorporating Cu nanoparticles with a volume fraction of 0.04 and a radius of 5 nm into Cu-water nanofluid, the maximum heat transfer by means of a revolving heat pipe at ΔT = 20°C and ω = 3000 rpm is enhanced by 56 %.
Assuming a constant mass of Nano-fluid containing particles of a certain size, the heat transfer is maximized at high pipe rotation speeds and concentrations of Nano-particles.
Raising the volume percentage of GO in comparison to the concentration of MoS2 results in an improvement in heat transmission.
After 200 s of WHP and stir bar acceleration, the nanofluid temperature drops 0.5°C, whereas the necessary nanofluid temperature rises 2.1°C under the same circumstances.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study provides a comprehensive overview of the effectiveness of nanofluids (NFs) in enhancing heat pipe (HP) performance, a critical component in thermal management applications such as electronics cooling, solar power conversion, and automotive systems. Traditional heat transfer fluids suffer from low thermal conductivity and heat transfer coefficients, causing a clear reduction of thermal efficacy. This review specifically addresses these limitations of traditional heat transfer fluids used in heat pipes by analysing both experimental and computational research from the open literature. Findings indicate that a the combination of nanoparticles, such as Al2O3, CuO, and carbon nanotubes can substantially enhance the thermal conductivity, mitigate thermal interface resistance, and boost the heat transfer coefficients. Additionally, factors such as nanoparticle concentration, particle size, and heat pipe geometry significantly influence overall thermal performance. Specifically, optimum nanoparticle concentrations (0.1–0.3 wt.%) are vital, as excessive loading can deter performance. Also, the performance fluctuates across various HP types, with geometric modifications further improving thermal efficiency. Even though short-term advantages are apparent, long-term stability concerns, such as nanoparticle agglomeration, necessitate future research. This study demonstrates the potential of nanofluids in heat pipes to advance next-generation thermal management technologies across different applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: two-phase closed thermo-siphon or tri-pass heat exchanger

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive heat may hinder performance or even cause failure in various engineering applications, including electronic devices and aeronautical systems. Therefore, effective thermal management is of paramount importance. An emerging and promising solution in passive heat transfer systems is the heat pipe (HP), which allows for substantial thermal energy transmission with nominal temperature gradients between the thermal source and the cooling sink. Phase transitions in the working medium lie at the core of HP operation: heat is absorbed in the evaporator, transferred through the unit, and finally heat is removed in the condenser [1]. To achieve optimal thermal performance (TP), this process depends on optimised fluid flow control and management which includes selecting an appropriate fluid, setting the correct pipe inclination, and determining optimal filling ratios [2]. Metal oxide nanoparticles, originating from metals, i.e. iron, copper, and aluminium, are widely used in nanofluid (NF) applications [3,4]. The base fluid in an NF may be H2O, oil ethylene glycol, and specific mixture of fluid and nanoparticles depends on the intended application. Experiments have shown that thermal conductivity (TC) of NFs can be significantly improved (by 5 % to 10 %) through the addition of nanoparticles, even at low volume fractions [5–12]. This enhancement is attributed to particle motion and dispersion effects, which improve heat transfer. NFs are thus considered effective heat transfer media in advanced cooling systems such as nuclear reactors and cylindrical HPs [13]. Their ability to improve TC of working fluids makes NFs promising candidates for high-performance applications [14]. NF-based coolants using particles such as Si, Al2O3, CuO, and TiO2 are suitable for heat exchangers, electronic cooling devices, and radiators or other systems that transfer heat [15]. According to Kumar et al. [16], a 45° inclination yields optimal performance in copper nanoparticle-sintered HPs. Cylindrical and flat-plate HPs perform best with silver-based NFs containing particles smaller than 200 nm. In tests involving cylindrical HPs charged with 1 wt.% deionized water and nanoparticles of Al2O3, CuO, and ZnO, CuO NFs demonstrated superior performance at a 60° inclination [17]. Several studies also indicate improved TP with NF concentrations as low as 0.1 wt.% [18]. Another significant challenge is modifying the geometry of HPs without compromising heat transfer efficiency. For example, incorporating converging and diverging segments within the adiabatic region of a long Two -phase closed thermo-syphon (TPCT) improved its performance at a 45° inclination compared to conventional TPCTs. This improvement is attributed to reduced fluid accumulation, enhanced interaction between the convergent/divergent sections, and lower pressure drop. Flow pattern changes confirmed a 2 % increase in heat transfer efficiency with the modified TPCT design [19]. TPCTs are compact and suitable for long-distance heat transport, often placing the evaporator section at the bottom to take advantage of gravity [20]. TPCTs are ideal for cooling applications in aerospace, electronics, and solar power systems due to their compactness and flexibility [21]. Parameters such as energy input, type of working medium, filling ratio, and tilt angle have all been extensively studied to optimise heat transfer [22–24]. In the construction industry, circular and flat-plate HPs are also employed in walls and roofs to maintain stable indoor temperatures. This environmentally friendly cooling approach helps reduce reliance on air conditioning systems [25]. The outcomes of employing NFs in various HPs were reviewed and investigated by Singh and Gupta [26]. In particular, the issue of NF stability within HPs was emphasized. Compared to other working fluids, NFs were found to significantly improve the TP of HPs. However, NF stability remains a major challenge. In HP systems, operational constraints are critical for ensuring safe and efficient performance. Pandey and Gupta [27] examined how HPs utilising mono nanofluids and hybrid nanofluids as working fluids can enhance TP. To aid readers in understanding the influence of various design and operational parameters, the authors provided a concise summary of relevant literature. Similarly, Udayvir and Gupta [28] analysed both operational constraints and the effects of NFs on the TP of HPs. Their findings indicated that using NFs as the working medium generally improves HP performance. Key factors influencing HP TP include the type of operating fluid, pipe orientation, nanoparticle concentration and size, and power input. Thermo-physical properties, operating temperature range, and internal pressure also impact operational limitations. Notable constraints include boiling point, entrainment (pick-up), viscosity, sonication, and capillary limit [29].

Several review papers on the use of NFs in HPs can be found in the open literature. For instance, Wang et al. [29] identified three critical aspects of mono and hybrid NF usage in HPs: viscosity, thermal conductivity, and stability. Their review introduced different types of HPs and summarised the mechanisms that either promote or inhibit heat transfer. They also discussed the use of machine learning to simulate NF properties, such as thermal conductivity and dynamic viscosity, and its integration into HP systems. Another comprehensive review was carried out by Ghorabaee et al. [30], who sought to enhance the comprehension of fundamental heat transfer processes in thermo-syphon heat pipes using NFs. They investigated the effects of energy input, nanoparticle type, and particle concentration on the thermal behaviour of these systems. The review concluded that TP depends heavily on operational factors, especially nanoparticle type and concentration, surfactant presence, and heat input. A significant correlation was found between the nanofluid type and HP TP under variable heat sources. Finally, Jayanthi et al. [31] provided an in-depth evaluation of recent developments and future research directions for improving the efficiency of heat pipe solar collectors using NF technologies. The researchers discussed the percentage improvement in heat transfer achievable under optimal conditions and emphasized the potential of replacing base fluids with nanofluids to improve thermal efficiency in HP systems.

Although the growing body of literature on the use of NFs to enhance HP performance, a considerable gap remains in the existed reviews. The aforementioned studies (discussed above) have ascertained on individual aspects of NF properties, such as viscosity, thermal conductivity, and stability, without giving a complete vision of how these factors can interact across various HP setups besides no specific evaluation of long-term stability and performance variations across different HP types and geometries. Thus, the current review intends to fill this gap in the open literature by conducting a systematic analysis of both experimental and computational investigations on the use of NFs in HPs. This would address the existed limitations of traditional heat transfer fluids. The motivation behind this work stems from the request to associate findings under consistent criteria, assuring an obvious perception of optimum nanoparticle concentrations, the impact of HP geometry, and the potential for long-term stability and associated concerns such as Nanoparticle agglomeration. By synthesising the exited research, this review attempts to deliver valuable visions into the operative application of NFs in thermal management systems, eventually improving the field and directing future research. Referring to the scope under investigation, this review would introduce the case of thermo-syphon HPs, where the researchers investigated improvements in thermal characteristics through the use of NFs, taking into account the effects of nanoparticle volume fraction and size. Also, the research on low amplitude oscillating HPs with specific flow characteristics and heat transfer behaviours that can be observed when using NFs, is addressed, which are particularly beneficial in applications requiring rapid temperature changes. Furthermore, studies on screen mesh HPs are discussed and their possibility of enhancing capillary driving forces and convective heat transfer coefficients due to incorporating NFs. Lastly, in rotating HPs, centripetal forces are depicted to improve the TP of NF-based systems. Collectively, these categories underscore both the broad applicability of nanofluids across diverse HP configurations and the emerging avenues for future research and technological advancement in HP technology and systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improvement of HP efficiency using NF
ce:section.ce:para: Heat pipe efficiency has been enhanced by incorporating NFs into base fluids, with nanoparticles such as CuO and Al2O3 being commonly used. This enhancement reduces thermal resistance, thereby enabling heat pipes to operate more effectively under varying heat loads and temperature conditions [32]. Nanoparticles also improve the wettability of HP surfaces, leading to better liquid film formation and enhanced heat transfer. As a result, NFs offer significant potential for improving the TP of HPs in applications such as electronics cooling, automotive systems, and renewable energy technologies. However, challenges remain, including nanoparticle stability, material cost, and potential environmental impacts, which must be addressed to fully realize the widespread adoption of NFs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies related to thermo-syphon heat pump
ce:section.ce:para: Chen et al. [33] employed a novel water-based SiO2-functionalized NF as the working fluid in a loop thermo-syphon. Functionalized NFs exhibit enhanced dispersion stability and do not cause deposition on heated surfaces. To isolate the influence of the NF’s thermal properties on the heat transfer characteristics of the loop thermo-syphon—independent of the effects of the heated surface—the study focused on the flow boiling heat transfer behaviour using the functionalized NF. The results showed that thermal resistance (R) initially decreases steadily with increasing heat flux (Q) for water and a functionalized nanofluid at a fixed surface temperature (Ts) of 40°C but began to rise at higher heat flux levels. The decrease of R can be attributed to improved heat transfer due to enhanced fluid mobility and nanoparticle circulation and interactions. This leads to a greater heat transfer coefficient. However, thermal resistance can also rise at higher heat flux levels due to increased viscosity parameter and nanoparticle agglomeration in heat pipes. More importantly, water showed greater thermal resistance compared to functionalized nanofluid, which indicates its superior thermal performance until the onset of diminishing returns at elevated fluxes. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the thermal resistance of the functionalized NF was found to be 22–25 % higher than that of water.

Sarafraz et al. [34] experimentally examined the TP of a wickless thermo-syphon HP charged with an environmentally friendly working fluid synthesized through biological methods. The researchers used a green synthesis process, combining fresh tea leaf extract with aqueous silver nitrate to produce silver nanoparticles. A smooth copper tube measuring 280 mm in length, with an inner diameter of 10.7 mm and an outer diameter of 12 mm, was used to construct the HP. The experimental study included an analysis of various operational parameters influencing TP and efficiency of the HP. These factors included the applied heat flux to the evaporator, the working medium filling ratio, pipe inclination, nanoparticle concentration, and thermal response time. Notably, the results demonstrated that nanoparticle concentration significantly affects the temperature distribution along the HP length. Fig. 4 A shows the decline of temperature profile in the HP from the evaporator to the condenser, highlighting efficient heat transfer. In this regard, a noticeable temperature reduction in the evaporator section is assured as a result to increasing the concentration of the Ag/water nanofluid. This can be due to greater thermal conductivity and heat transfer coefficient of the nanofluid in a comparison to distilled water. Indeed, the existence of silver nanoparticles can facilitate better thermal energy absorption and transfer, which results in lower evaporator temperatures. Also, the effective surface area for heat exchange can be improved with increasing nanofluid concentration, which can lead to leading to more effective heat absorption in the evaporator and subsequently a steeper temperature gradient along the heat pipe. In other words, Fig. 4 shows that a more pronounced temperature reduction in the evaporator section was observed as the nanofluid concentration increased.

Sarafraz and Hormozi [35] investigated the effects of Al2O3-based NFs on the TP and efficiency of a thermo-syphon HP charged with Water–EG and Water–DEG, respectively. The HP was constructed from a high-quality, straight copper tube measuring 220 mm in length, with an external diameter of 12 mm. A range of operating parameters was experimentally analyzed and briefly discussed in relation to HP TP and efficiency. These parameters included the applied heat flux to the evaporator, tilt angle of the HP, volumetric concentration of nanoparticles, and the fluid filling ratio. The results demonstrated that employing NFs as working fluids significantly enhanced the heat transfer coefficient and, consequently, the thermal efficiency of the HP. Specifically, the alumina, Water/EG nanofluids exhibited lower thermal resistance and higher heat transfer coefficients compared to alumina, Water/DEG nanofluids (Fig. 5 ).

To investigate the influence of an external electric field on the TP of a two-phase closed thermo-syphon, Heris et al. [36] conducted a series of experimental studies. Two distinct water-based NFs, prepared by ultrasonically dispersing CuO and Al2O3 nanoparticles in deionized water, were used as working fluids. The results showed that increasing both the electric field intensity and nanoparticle concentration led to progressive improvements in the Nusselt number ratio and overall TP of the thermo-syphon. However, these performance metrics declined as the input power increased. Among the tested fluids, the Al2O3/water nanofluid enhanced TP more effectively than the CuO/water nanofluid. Furthermore, the study indicated that nanoparticle concentration had a more pronounced effect on the thermo-syphon’s TP than the applied electric field. As both nanoparticle concentration and electric field intensity increased, so did the thermal efficiency of the two-phase closed thermo-syphon. Fig. 6 illustrates that Al2O3 nanoparticles contributed to a greater improvement in thermal efficiency at various electric field strengths compared to CuO nanoparticles. Al2O3 nanoparticles have greater thermal conductivity and better dispersion in the base fluid compared to CuO nanoparticles, which reasons more efficient heat transfer. The interaction between Al2O3 nanoparticles and the working fluid is better than CuO nanoparticles due to its surface characteristics and morphology.

Amiri et al. [37] employed both covalent and non-covalent functionalization methods to disperse graphene Nanoplatelets (GNPs) in aqueous media. To evaluate the thermo-physical and rheological properties, water-based NFs containing various concentrations of GNPs were prepared. Among the tested fluids, GNP-COOH/water NFs outperformed both GNP–SDBS (Sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate)/water NFs and deionized water in a thermo-syphon, particularly in terms of rheological parameters—such as effective viscosity and total pressure drop—and thermal properties, including net heat transfer and thermal efficiency. The results indicated that increasing both the input power and GNP concentration led to a corresponding rise in the total heat transfer coefficient. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the covalently functionalized GNP-COOH samples exhibited the highest increase in heat transfer coefficient compared to the other formulations. Specifically, an input power of 149.8 W combined with a GNP-COOH concentration of 0.1 % resulted in the maximum observed heat transfer coefficient of 601 W/m2°C. This behaviour can be attributed to the enhancement of thermal conductivity and dispersion stability due to the existence of covalently functionalized GNP-COOH, which minimises agglomeration and maximises thermal contact.

Using an innovative and cost-effective exfoliation technique, Soleymaniha et al. [38] synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Morphological characterization of the synthesized amine-treated GQDs (AGQDs) revealed an average thickness of less than 1 nm and a diameter in the range of 5–20 nm. Experimental investigations demonstrated that water-based AGQD NFs exhibited significantly higher thermal conductivity than the base fluid, even at very low weight fractions. Compared to distilled water, AGQD–water NFs showed markedly improved performance in a thermo-syphon system, particularly in terms of thermal efficiency, effective viscosity, and total pressure drop. As shown in Fig. 8 , the total heat transfer coefficient increased substantially when 0.002 wt.% of AGQDs are dispersed in water. Specifically, the average total heat transfer coefficient of the thermo-syphon increased by 38.14 % and 24.20 % with the addition of 0.002 wt.% and 0.001 wt.% AGQDs, respectively, in deionized water.

To evaluate the heat transfer coefficient, thermal resistance, and overall TP of a thermo-syphon HP charged with a zirconia–acetone NF, Sarafraz et al. [39] conducted a comprehensive experimental study. The experiments were specifically designed to operate under high heat flux conditions, up to 200 kW/m², where the thermo-syphon could potentially match or even exceed the performance of evacuated solar tube heat pipes. The zirconia–acetone NF was prepared by adding a surfactant, sonicating to ensure homogeneous dispersion, and adjusting the pH using a buffer solution. The thermo-syphon used in the study had an axial length of 280 mm, with external and internal diameters of 12 mm and 10.7 mm, respectively, and was fabricated from oxygen-free copper. Importantly, the results demonstrated that, at the maximum applied heat flux, the presence of the nanofluid significantly reduced the total thermal resistance of the HP, reaching its lowest value. Additionally, the heat transfer coefficient in the evaporator section improved notably. The highest heat transfer coefficient was achieved at a 60 % filling ratio and a tilt angle of 65°. As shown in Fig. 9 , Kiseev and Sazhin [40] focused on two-phase thermal control systems, specifically loop thermo-syphons (LTS) containing nanofluids, and their potential application in LED cooling devices. The researchers presented a diagrammatic representation of the LTS and examined various influencing factors, including nanofluid aging, preparation methods, and nanoparticle dispersion. Their findings, derived from a series of experiments, indicated that nanoparticle mass concentration, shape, filling volume, and the thermo-physical properties of the working fluid significantly affect thermo-syphon performance. The study also demonstrated that nanofluids can substantially enhance heat transfer in LTS systems, with improvements in the heat transfer coefficient reaching 20–25 %. Moreover, the formation of Micro/Nano structures on the vaporization chamber surface was observed, attributed to nanoparticle aggregation during operation. Fig. 10 illustrates a notable increase in the evaporating heat transfer coefficient (LTS1) corresponding to nanoparticle mass concentrations of 0, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 wt.%.

Sardarabadi et al. [41] investigated the TP of functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT)/water NFs using a novel thermo-syphon design. The system consisted of a square-shaped evaporator and a condenser made of two interconnected tubes. The MWCNT-based NFs were characterized using a thermal conductivity analyzer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy. The characterization results indicated that nanofluids functionalized with sodium groups exhibited superior thermal conductivity and stability. Incorporating sodium-functionalized NFs in the thermo-syphon led to reduced thermal resistance and enhanced thermal efficiency. Experimental findings further revealed that the thermo-syphon charged with nanofluid could operate at higher input powers (as high as 90 W) compared to one charged with water (up to 70 W).

In a separate experimental study, Kaya [42] explored the TP of a tri-pass heat exchanger-based 2-phase closed thermo-syphon (TPCT) using CuO nanoparticles at 1 wt.% and 2 wt.% concentrations. The experimental setup included a copper pipe 100 cm in length with an inner diameter of 18 mm. The TPCT consisted of an evaporator (0.4 m), adiabatic section (0.2 m), and condenser (0.4 m), all of which were thermally insulated. An electrical spiral heater was wrapped around the evaporator to provide heat, while a cooling water circuit was constructed to dissipate heat from the condenser. The TPCT was operated at a fixed tilt angle of 90°. TP was evaluated under varying conditions, including heat input levels (200–800 W), cooling water flow rates (18–54 L/h), and different working fluids. Replacing pure water with CuO/water nanofluids improved TPCT performance. Specifically, nanofluids containing 1 wt.% and 2 wt.% CuO nanoparticles increased TP by approximately 10 % and 18.5 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 11 .

To study the thermal resistance and flow dynamics of TPCT containing NF, Wang et al. [43] proposed a two-dimensional steady-state model. Specifically, the model employed the dichotomy method to solve the energy, momentum, and mass conservation equations. Numerical methods were used to investigate the thermodynamics and entropy generation in the thermo-syphon under varying input powers, nanoparticle materials (Cu, Al2O3, and Fe2O3), and volume concentrations (φ = 0-12 wt.%). Results were compared to those for a system using only water. The inclusion of NFs significantly enhanced TP by reducing the evaporator temperature, resulting in decreased total entropy generation by 41 % for Cu, 32 % for Fe2O3, and 29 % for Al2O3, relative to clean water. Entropy generation also decreased substantially as nanoparticle concentration increased. However, the numerical results indicated that at φ = 9 wt.%, the maximum frictional entropy generation increased by 9.09 % for Cu, 36.37 % for Fe2O3, and 15.15 % for Al2O3, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 12 , a more pronounced reduction in thermal resistance was observed when copper nanoparticles were dispersed in the base fluid, compared to Fe2O3 and Al2O3. This behaviour can be attributed to the greater thermal conductivity of copper nanoparticles compared to Fe2O3 and Al2O3, which permits for more effective heat transfer. Also, the copper nanoparticles of the smallest size can enhance the interaction with the base fluid, which promotes better heat conduction.

Baek et al. [44] experimentally investigated the effects of thermal conductivity and surface tension on the performance of a TPCT using distilled water (DW), 0.5 wt.% Al2O3 NF, and either no surfactant or 1.5 wt.% sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS). The TP of the TPCT was assessed under various conditions, including different heat input powers (5, 10, and 15 W) and filling ratios (30 %, 50 %, and 70 %). The results indicated that reducing the surface tension of the working fluid by approximately 6.7 % could lower thermal resistance, provided the thermal conductivities of the Al2O3 NF and DW were nearly identical. Interestingly, the TPCT's thermal resistance decreased even when surface tension was reduced by 58.7 % and thermal conductivity decreased by 2.1 %. In contrast, when surface tension decreased by 58.3 % and thermal conductivity by 5.6 %, the thermal resistance increased. Furthermore, for all tested filling ratios and heat input levels, the comparative thermal resistance ratio of the Al2O3 NF with SDBS was greater than 1.0 (Fig. 13 ). Specifically, at a 30 % filling ratio, the thermal resistance of the Al2O3 NF with SDBS was 53 %, 103 %, and 204 % higher than that of the Al2O3 NF without SDBS at heat inputs of 5 W, 10 W, and 15 W, respectively.

Saafan [45] conducted both theoretical and experimental investigations on the TP of thermo-syphons for managing high heat fluxes. The working fluid in the evaporator was either a nanofluid composed of Al2O3 and water or clean water. In the theoretical model, the flow was characterized by the continuity, momentum, and energy equations, under the assumption of two-dimensional, steady, laminar flow with constant properties. The governing differential equations were solved numerically using the finite difference method. The results indicated improved TP when 0.8 % volume concentration of Al2O3 nanoparticles was added to water, compared to the use of pure water. Specifically, the overall thermal resistance decreased with increasing nanoparticle concentration from 0 % to 0.8 %, however, it began to increase again beyond 0.8 % up to 1.6 %. Thus, the optimal nanoparticle concentration for thermal enhancement was identified as 0.8 %.

Wang et al. [46] introduced an innovative study on the influence of nanofluid mechanisms on the performance of a flat confined loop thermo-syphon (FCLTS), including the feasibility of using ternary hybrid nanofluids (HNFs) in heat pipes. The researchers developed HNFs containing alumina, copper, and titanium dioxide by varying mixing ratios and concentrations. The study primarily focused on the effects of heat loads, inclination angles, and nanofluid concentrations. A notable finding was that nanofluid attachment to the upper heated surface of the evaporator, above the liquid level, improved surface wettability. Due to gravity-assisted effects, a 90° inclination angle yielded optimal performance. The ideal HNF concentration for balancing thermal resistance and enhancement was found to be 0.3 vol.%. At this concentration, under an 80 W heat load and a 90° inclination angle, the FCLTS achieved the lowest thermal resistance (0.09 K/W) and the highest heat transfer enhancement efficiency (22.5 %).

Dhairiyasamy et al. [47] evaluated the influence of coated silver Nanoplates on the thermal efficacy of copper heat pipe under variable operating conditions, particularly concentrating on the optimisation of nanofluids for improved thermal performance. The research comprised the synthesis of silver Nanoplates with controlled morphologies and plasmonic resonance peaks. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and silica coatings were deployed to stabilize the Nanoplates. The prepared nanofluids were experienced in a copper heat pipe across various filling ratios of 60 % to 90 % and inclination angles of 0° to 90°. The thermal resistance and heat transfer coefficients were calculated under variable heat inputs. The findings demonstrated the maximum heat transfer coefficient of 362 W/m² K was attained using 90 % filling ratio of PVP-coated Nanoplates and 90° inclination. This is compared to 350 W/m² K for distilled water. The silica coatings indicated a superior long-term stability, which maintains more than 98 % of their initial dispersion quality after thermal cycling. A summary of studies related to NF use in thermo-syphon heat pipes is presented in Table 1 .

Qu and Wu [48] experimentally investigated the thermal resistance of two identical oscillating heat pipes (OHPs) charged with Al2O3/water and SiO2/water NFs, respectively. At a 50 % volume filling ratio, the average temperature of the evaporator wall and the total thermal resistance of the OHPs were measured and compared across various nanoparticle mass concentrations (0–1.2 wt.% for Al2O3 NFs and 0–0.6 wt.% for SiO2 NFs). The results revealed that the TP of the OHPs was influenced differently depending on the type of nanofluid used. As shown in Fig. 14 , within the tested concentration ranges, the use of Al2O3 NFs in place of water improved HTP. This improvement manifested as a 5.6 °C (8.7 %) reduction in the evaporator wall temperature and a decrease in thermal resistance to 0.057 °C/W, a 25.7 % reduction. In contrast, using SiO2 NFs in place of water negatively impacted TP, resulting in a 3.5 °C (5.5 %) increase in evaporator wall temperature and a thermal resistance of 0.075 °C/W, representing a 23.7 % increase.

Tanshen et al. [49] investigated how varying concentrations of aqueous NFs based on MWCNTs affect heat transfer and the associated pressure distribution in OHPs. A multi-loop OHP was used to study the heat transfer behavior, and it was charged with aqueous NFs containing MWCNTs at concentrations of 0.05 wt.%, 0.1 wt.%, 0.2 wt.%, and 0.3 wt.%. The experimental setup consisted of a multi-loop OHP with an internal diameter of 3 mm and a filling ratio of 60 %. Fig. 15 illustrates that temporal pressure variations are a key determinant of TP and that thermal properties are closely correlated to pressure distribution. According to this research, the OHP charged with 0.2 wt.% MWCNT nanofluid exhibited the lowest thermal resistance and the highest frequency of pressure fluctuations, regardless of the heat input to the evaporator.

In their experimental study on a magnetically activated copper OHP, Goshayeshi et al. [50] investigated the use of an Fe2O3/kerosene NF. As a control, the researchers first evaluated the heat pipe’s temperature distribution and heat transfer rate without applying a magnetic field. By subjecting the Fe2O3 nanoparticles to a magnetic field, the vapor temperature could also be directly measured at the OHP’s central core. The findings demonstrated that both the heat transfer coefficient and thermal resistance of the OHP improved with the inclusion of Fe2O3 nanoparticles, particularly when influenced by a magnetic field. As the input heat flux increased, the heat transfer coefficients of both the condenser and evaporator also increased. In the evaporator section, the temperature difference between the vapor core and the surface was 3.1 °C without the magnetic field and 2 °C with it. Accordingly, it can be said that a significant thermal performance of heat pipes can be assured using nanofluids and the magnetic fields.

Su et al. [51] examined the experimental performance of an OHP charged with a self-rewetting nanofluid. The self-rewetting nanofluid was prepared by mixing a graphene oxide dispersion (the NF) with a self-rewetting fluid, an aqueous solution of n-butanol. A straight copper tube with a length of 600 mm and an internal diameter of 2 mm was used to construct the OHP. The research explored how the HTP of the OHP varied with different mass fractions of nanoparticles and n-butanol. Experimental results identified an optimal composition, at which the OHP achieved peak TP. At this optimum concentration, the self-rewetting nanofluid achieved a maximum heat transfer enhancement ratio of approximately 16 %, which was significantly higher than that achieved using the nanofluid or the self-rewetting fluid alone. As shown in Fig. 16 , the enhancement ratio initially increases with the heat load, reaches a peak, and then decreases as the heat load continues to rise.

Zhou et al. [52] conducted experimental investigations into the HTP of OHPs charged with graphene Nanoplatelet (GNP) NFs. The experiments were carried out across a range of heating powers (10–100 W), filling ratios (45 %, 55 %, 62 %, 70 %, and 90 %), and GNP concentrations (1.2, 2.0, 5.7, 9.1, 13.8, and 16.7 vol.%). Compared to OHPs charged with deionized water (DW), those using GNP NFs showed enhanced HTP. The optimal GNP concentration range (2.0–13.8 vol.%) yielded the best results at filling ratios of 55 %, 62 %, and 70 %. Notably, at 2.0 vol.% GNP concentration and a 62 % filling ratio, the thermal resistance decreased by up to 83.6 % compared to the DW-based OHP under a heating power of 80 W. Furthermore, adding GNP NFs at a low filling ratio (45 %) was effective in improving heat transfer and mitigating dry-out.

Jin et al. [53] explored the use of directional absorptive nanofluids in OHPs for solar energy applications, aiming to enhance solar energy absorption and transport without requiring additional pumping power. The study examined several parameters, including nanoparticle type and concentration, nanofluid filling ratio, and solar radiation intensity, and found that an optimal filling ratio of 83 % enabled both minimal thermal resistance and single-directional circulation of the working fluid. When the OHP was charged with 3.0 wt.% MWCNT nanofluid, an exceptionally high thermal conductivity of 6,000 W/m K was achieved. Under experimental conditions, the system reached a maximum energy conversion efficiency of 92 %.

To ensure safe thermal management of electric vehicle batteries during rapid charging and discharging, Zhou et al. [54] developed a hybrid OHP composed of a copper flat-plate evaporator with parallel circular channels and a capillary copper-tube condenser. The device was designed to dissipate battery-generated heat effectively. As working fluids, ethanol–water mixtures (1:1 by volume) with carbon nanotubes (CNTs) at mass concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 0.5 wt.% were used, with a volumetric filling ratio of 35 %. Experimental results showed that compared to the ethanol–water mixture alone, the CNT-enhanced nanofluid improved both start-up performance and HTP. As shown in Fig. 17 , at a CNT concentration of 0.2 wt.% and a heat input of 56 W, the average evaporator temperature dropped to 43.1 °C, and the thermal resistance decreased to 0.066 °C/W—representing reductions of 9.8 °C and 0.278 °C/W, respectively, compared to the baseline ethanol–water mixture.

Zhang et al. [55] enhanced the performance of their proposed device by incorporating Fe3O4 NPs into the working fluid of a nanoscale OHP. The effects of nanoparticle size and an external magnetic field were analysed by observing peak values of density, velocity, temperature, and heat flux after 20 nanoseconds. According to the simulation results, increasing the NP size from 5 nm to 10 nm led to an increase in heat flux from 1561 W/m2 to 1602 W/m2. This indicates that the Fe3O4-H2O nanofluid exhibited enhanced thermal behavior with increasing nanoparticle radius. Additionally, the results demonstrated that the Nanofluid’s TP in the OHP improved in the presence of an external magnetic field. As the magnetic field strength increased from 1 T to 5 T, the heat flux rose from 1563 W/m2 to 1586 W/m2.
Table 2 provides a summary of studies related to the application of nanofluids in OHPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies related to screen mesh HP
ce:section.ce:para: In their experimental investigation, Do et al. [56] tested circular screen mesh wick HPs using water-based Al2O3 NFs at volume fractions of 1.0 % and 3.0 % to evaluate the influence of NFs on TP. The researchers compared thermal resistance and wall temperature distributions between the adiabatic and evaporator sections of HPs charged with nanofluids and those charged with DW. Compared to HPs using DW, those employing water-based Al2O3 NFs exhibited significantly lower average evaporator wall temperatures. As shown in Fig. 18 , the thermal resistance at the evaporator–adiabatic interface decreased by approximately 40 % when using a 3.0 vol.% Al2O3 nanofluid. This can be ascribed to can be attributed to the improved thermal conductivity and heat transfer features of the Al2O3 nanofluid. The results further indicated that replacing DW with Al2O3 NFs as the working fluid can increase the maximum heat transfer rate of the HP.

Using extended sonication and homogenization, Kole and Dey [57] synthesised stable copper–deionized water nanofluids (Cu–DW NFs) without the use of surfactants. At 30 °C, the nanofluid exhibited a maximum enhancement of approximately 15 % in thermal conductivity at a Cu concentration of 0.5 wt.%. These results were compared with those obtained using pure water as the working fluid in an identical HP. As heat input increases, wall temperatures in the HP decrease along the evaporator section and rise toward the condenser. Compared to HPs filled with DW alone, those using Cu–DW NFs exhibited significantly lower average evaporator wall temperatures. Additionally, while the thermal resistance of the HP containing the nanofluid was initially higher at low heat loads, it rapidly decreased as the heat load increased. As shown in Fig. 19 , the thermal resistance of a vertically oriented HP charged with 0.5 wt.% Cu–DW NF was reduced by approximately 27 %.

Asirvatham et al. [58] demonstrated an enhancement in HP TP by dispersing silver nanoparticles into DW. These HPs, suitable for dissipating heat from power transistors in computer electronics and processors, were tested under heat inputs ranging from 20 W to 100 W in five stages. The researchers experimentally investigated the influence of several operating conditions and test parameters, including nanoparticle volume fraction, vapor temperature, and heat input, on thermal resistance as well as the evaporation and condensation heat transfer coefficients. The silver nanoparticles used in the study had an average diameter of 58.35 nm, and their volume concentration varied from 0.003 % to 0.009 %. Notably, at a concentration of 0.009 vol.%, the silver nanoparticles reduced thermal resistance by 76.2 %. As shown in Fig. 20 , this concentration also led to a 52.7 % improvement in the heat transfer coefficients. The use of silver nanoparticles extended the HP’s operational range by approximately 21 % compared to DW alone. These improvements can be attributed to the excellent thermal conductivity of silver and its ability to improve fluid dynamics.

In their two-dimensional transient numerical model, Solomon et al. [59] predicted key thermal and fluid flow parameters in a screen mesh wick HP, including vapor core temperature, wall temperature, vapor pressure, vapor velocity, and liquid velocity. Numerical solutions were obtained for the energy, momentum, and mass conservation equations in both the vapor and liquid phases. Using the developed model, the researchers examined the impact of a copper–water NF on the HTP of the HP and compared the results with those using DW. The model also identified the dry-out location in the evaporator section and provided transient profiles of vapor and liquid velocities within the wick structure. The results indicated that the addition of copper nanoparticles reduced operating pressure, vapor temperature, wall temperature, and overall thermal resistance, leading to improved heat transfer capacity under constant heat load conditions. As shown in Fig. 21 , under identical operating conditions, the HP charged with 0.1 wt.% copper–water NF exhibited vapor and liquid velocities approximately 20 % higher than those observed in the DW-based HP.

Ghanbarpour et al. [61] investigated the impact of silver-based NFs on the TP of inclined screen mesh HPs for cooling applications. In this experiment, DW and water-based silver NFs at mass concentrations of 0.25 %, 0.5 %, and 0.75 % were used to evaluate four cylindrical copper HPs, each fitted with two layers of screen mesh wicks. The tests were conducted at four different inclination angles: 0°, 30°, 60°, and 90°. The experimental results showed that HPs using silver NFs performed better than those using only DW, with thermal resistance decreasing as nanoparticle concentration increased. The performance was also influenced by gravitational effects; TP peaked at a 60° inclination angle, outperforming the other tested angles. As illustrated in Fig. 24 , the average improvement in effective thermal conductivity for inclined HPs compared to the horizontal configuration was approximately 7 % at 30°, 11 % at 60°, and 4 % at 90°.

Ghanbarpour et al. [62] investigated the effect of an Al2O3 NF on the cooling performance of a screen mesh HP. Three copper HPs, each 200 mm long and 6.35 mm in diameter, were fabricated with two layers of screen mesh. These were tested using both DW and water-based Al2O3 NFs at mass concentrations of 5 % and 10 %. Compared to DW, the results showed that TP improved with 5 wt.% Al2O3 NF, but deteriorated with the use of 10 wt.% Al2O3 NF. At increased heat fluxes, HPs containing 5 wt.% Al2O3 NF reduced thermal resistance by 6 % to 24 %, and decreased evaporator wall temperatures by 20 % to 55 %. In contrast, HPs with 10 wt.% Al2O3 NF exhibited a thermal resistance increase of 187 % to 206 % and a reduction in heat transfer efficiency by 155 % to 175 %.

Ramachandran et al. [63] experimentally studied the TP of cylindrical screen mesh HPs charged with HNFs. The HNFs were prepared by dispersing CuO and Al2O3 nanoparticles in DW. The heat pipe was constructed using a 300 mm long, straight copper tube with an outer diameter of 12.5 mm and a wall thickness of 1 mm. The wick structure consisted of three layers of 100-mesh copper screen. The heat input was applied in five equal increments, ranging from 50 W to 250 W. Three HNFs with different nanoparticle ratios were tested: 75 % Al2O3 / 25 % CuO, 50 % Al2O3 / 50 % CuO, and 25 % Al2O3 / 75 % CuO. All HNFs had a total nanoparticle volume concentration of 0.1 %. The study found that, compared to DW, the HNF with 25 % Al2O3 and 75 % CuO reduced thermal resistance by 44.25 % at the maximum heat load of 250 W. This reduction was attributed to the formation of a porous coating on the wick surface, which increased surface roughness and wettability. As a result, additional nucleation sites were formed, as observed in the SEM images presented in Fig. 25 .

To investigate how aqueous Al2O3 NFs affect the hydrothermal performance of a high-efficiency heat exchanger with separate heat sources (evaporators), Mashaei et al. [64] performed a numerical analysis. The study examined the effects of nanoparticle volume fractions (φ = 0 %, 0.075 %, 2.5 %, and 5 %) and heat loads (Q = 14, 28, 56, and 112 W) on velocity and temperature fields, pressure drop, and overall TP of the HP. Nanoparticles were found to have the most significant effect in reducing wall temperatures near the heat sources, where thermal loads are highest. Specifically, as the nanoparticle volume fraction increased, velocity within the wick structure decreased, while pressure drop increased. Moreover, the impact of nanoparticles on the hydraulic and TP of the HP became more pronounced with higher wick porosity and smaller particle sizes. The optimal performance was achieved at a volume fraction of φ = 5 % and a heat load of Q = 112 W.

In a related experimental study, Senthil et al. [65] investigated the influence of inclination angle on the thermal resistance and effectiveness of HPs using Al2O3 nanofluid and DW as working fluids. The performance metrics evaluated included the total heat transfer coefficient and thermal resistance, with variations in working fluid quantity. The nanofluid consisted of Al2O3 nanoparticles with a density of 9.8 g/cm³ at a concentration of 1 vol.%. Experiments were conducted under varying thermal loads and inclination angles. Thermocouples were used to monitor temperature distribution throughout the tests. As shown in Fig. 26 , the results demonstrate that HPs charged with Al2O3 nanofluids exhibit improved TP and are suitable for practical heat exchanger applications.

Herrera et al. [66] conducted a study on the impact of nanoparticle concentration in an Al2O3/water NF on the capillary limit of a HP using a screen mesh wick. The flow and phase-change behavior of the working fluid within the wick structure were described by a set of hydrodynamic equations, which were incorporated into a numerical model. The addition of nanoparticles to the working fluid was found to raise the capillary limit, allowing the HP to sustain higher heat input before reaching that limit. An improvement of 30–40 % in the capillary limit was identified as optimal. As shown in Fig. 27 , in the absence of nanoparticles, thermal resistance was higher and capillary limit enhancement was less consistent, particularly at elevated nanoparticle concentrations. The study concluded that the improvement in capillary limit is primarily due to enhanced wick wettability, rather than changes in effective pore radius or thermal conductivity. The optimal nanoparticle concentration for delaying the onset of the capillary limit was determined to be 0.5 wt.% Al2O3/water.

Channapattana et al. [67] investigated the effect of multilayer screen mesh wicks and CuO NFs on the TP of HPs. To enhance capillary action, three HPs were fabricated with varying screen mesh wick thicknesses. Each HP was 350 mm in length and 19.5 mm in diameter. Mesh configurations tested included 100+150, 100+2 × 150, and 4 × 150. The CuO nanoparticle concentration was maintained at 1 wt.% in water. Both water and CuO NFs were used as working fluids under varying heat inputs of 35 W, 50 W, 75 W, 100 W, and 120 W. Thermal resistance decreased with increasing heat input. Compared to DW, the CuO NFs provided improved heat transfer rate (HTR). Furthermore, the overall heat transfer increased proportionally with the number of mesh layers, regardless of pipe orientation.

Herrera et al. [68] developed a phenomenological model to study nanoparticle interactions in Al2O3/water nanofluids within cylindrical mesh wick HPs. The model incorporated population balance equations based on Navier–Stokes equations to account for particle dynamics. The capillary limit (CL) values with nanofluids were 32 % higher than those with DW. While the theoretical improvement in CL was attributed to reduced pore radius and increased capillary pressure, experimental results at low NF concentrations suggested that other mechanisms, such as enhanced wettability, played a more significant role. The model also indicated that excessive nanoparticle concentrations could lead to deposition in the wick, thereby increasing thermal resistance.

Using silver (Ag) nanoparticles, Alphonse et al. [69] studied the effects of HP inclination angle and nanoparticle size on the TP of cylindrical screen mesh HPs. Particle sizes of 30 nm, 50 nm, and 80 nm were tested at inclination angles of 0°, 45°, 60°, and 90°. Thermal conductivity increased with greater inclination, with the maximum observed at 90° for all particle sizes. Additionally, thermal resistance decreased as particle size decreased. Thermal conductivities of 250, 200, and 150 W/m·K were recorded for particles of 30, 50, and 80 nm, respectively. Corresponding heat transfer coefficients were 5,500, 4,500, and 3,500 W/m2·K. For all particle sizes, the highest heat transfer coefficient occurred at a 90° inclination. The addition of 1 vol.% Ag nanoparticles significantly improved thermal performance and reduced thermal resistance.

Vidhya et al. [70] explored the potential of HNFs composed of MgO and Al2O3 to enhance the thermal performance of cylindrical mesh HPs. Compared to a water–ethylene glycol mixture, the MgO–Al2O3 HNF significantly improved performance by enhancing evaporator surface wettability and reducing thermal resistance. The nanofluid coating also improved the heat transfer coefficient. Response Surface Methodology (RSM) was employed to model the relationship between nanoparticle concentration, input power, and key thermal responses such as thermal resistance and heat transfer coefficient. As shown in Fig. 28 , the heat transfer coefficient increased with volume concentration. A peak heat transfer coefficient of 99.61 W/m2·K, representing a 129.93 % improvement over the base fluid, was achieved with 0.1 % nanoparticle concentration at a power input of 90 W.

Reddy et al. [71] investigated the effects of heat load, tilt angle, and alumina NF volume concentration on the thermal resistance (R) and thermal conductivity of a cylindrical screen mesh wick HP. A response surface methodology based on the Box–Behnken design was employed to analyze the influence of three independent variables: heat input (100–200 W), tilt angle (0–90°), and NF volume concentration (0.05–0.25 vol.%). Second-order polynomial equations were used to predict R and thermal conductivity, and analysis of variance (ANOVA) was applied to evaluate the statistical significance of the models. The optimal conditions for minimizing thermal resistance and maximizing thermal conductivity were identified as a heat input of 200 W, a tilt angle of 52.72°, and an NF concentration of 0.1773 vol.%. Table 3 provides a summary of relevant studies involving the use of nanofluids in screen mesh wick heat pipes.

Hamdy and Harmand [72] investigated the effect of a copper–water nanofluid (Cu–water NF) on heat transfer in a rotating heat pipe (RHP). A mathematical model of the RHP was developed, incorporating variables such as vapor velocity, gravitational effects, and taper angle. The study explored various values of rotational speed (ω), temperature difference across the RHP (ΔT), and working fluid mass. Compared to conventional fluids, the use of Cu–water nanofluid enhanced heat transfer and reduced the liquid film thickness along the pipe walls. The results also indicated that increasing the nanoparticle volume fraction, particle radius, and ΔT improved the overall heat transfer. Specifically, using Cu–water NF with a 0.04 vol.% concentration and 5 nm particle radius resulted in a 56 % increase in heat transfer at ΔT = 20 °C and ω = 3000 rpm.

Hassan and Harmand [73] further studied the effect of NFs on the performance of spinning heat pipes. Their investigation focused on the influence of NFs on heat transfer and liquid film thickness. Water was used as the base fluid, with three types of nanoparticles—Al2O3, Cu, and CuO—dispersed at varying volume fractions and particle radii. The mathematical model included parameters such as gravity, vapor velocity, and taper angle of the condenser. Experimental results showed that decreasing the condenser taper angle, along with increasing the nanoparticle volume fraction and particle size, led to improved heat transfer. Notably, the use of Cu–water NF with a 0.04 vol. % concentration and 5 nm particle radius produced a 56 % increase in maximum heat transfer at ω = 3000 rpm and ΔT = 20 °C. This result is clearly illustrated in Fig. 29 .

Through numerical analysis, Uddin et al. [74] investigated heat transfer in a spinning HP containing a NF. Several parameters were identified as influencing the HTP of the HP, including the NF mass, rotational speed (ω), nanoparticle concentration, and particle size. An optimal mass of the working fluid (i.e., the NF) was found to be necessary for maximizing heat transfer. The study showed that, for fixed nanoparticle size and concentration, heat transfer was maximized at lower NF mass and higher rotational speed. Additionally, when the concentration of small-sized nanoparticles in the base fluid was high, the best thermal performance was achieved for a specific combination of NF mass and ω. As illustrated in Fig. 30 , heat transfer was greatest at high nanoparticle concentrations and high rotational speeds, given a fixed NF mass and specific particle size.

Uddin et al. [75] conducted a sensitivity analysis using a statistical experimental design approach in conjunction with numerical modeling to study heat transfer in a rotating heat pipe (RHP). The working fluid used in the study was a HNF composed of graphene oxide (GO) and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2). The researchers examined the influence of key heat pipe parameters, initial mass, temperature differential, and rotational speed (ω), on heat transfer and liquid film thickness. The results demonstrated that the addition of hybrid nanoparticles enhances the thermal performance of the HP and supports the formation of a thinner liquid film. Heat transfer increased with decreasing inflow mass and temperature differential, while an increase in rotational speed caused a slight reduction in thermal performance. Moreover, an increase in the GO volume fraction relative to MoS2 further improved heat transfer. As shown in Fig. 31 , the analysis confirmed, with 95 % statistical significance, that the most influential linear factors enhancing heat transfer are the GO concentration in the HNF, the intake mass of the HNF, and the temperature difference across the evaporator section of the pipe.

In a study conducted by Alic [76], the evaporator of a wick heat pipe (WHP) was vertically positioned at the center of a NF vortex, while the condenser fins dissipated heat to the surrounding air. The NF, composed of Al2O3 nanoparticles dispersed in water, was pre-heated and then circulated to form a vortex inside a vertical cylindrical tank. By modulating the angular velocity of a stir bar located at the base of the vessel, the tangential velocity of the vortex was controlled. Using a non-evacuated wickless heat pipe under conditions of uneven rotational flow, the researchers measured NF temperatures and evaluated WHP efficiency within the vortex field. The results indicated that the heat exchange intensities at both the evaporator and condenser were influenced by the rotation of the WHP. After 200 s of WHP operation and stir bar acceleration, the NF temperature decreased by 0.5 °C, while the required NF temperature increased by 2.1 °C under the same conditions. Conversely, after 140 s of deceleration, the required NF temperature dropped by 3.8 %. A summary of studies related to the use of Nanofluids in wick heat pipes is presented in Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Critical analysis of utilising nanofluids in different types of heat pipes
ce:section.ce:para:
The current review discussed the potential improvements in the use of NFs for enhancing HP performance, despite the existence of several significant challenges that must be addressed to support proper implementation and commercialization. These challenges include:
1.
One of the primary issues associated with NFs is maintaining long-term stability of suspended nanoparticles [77–80]. Agglomeration and sedimentation due to particle clustering can degrade thermal conductivity and overall performance. Further research is needed to develop effective stabilization strategies, such as the use of surfactants or surface modification techniques.
2.
In the analysed studies and summarised in Tables 1–4, several studies in this field typically employed the numerical method of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and mesh resolutions for the 3D modelling. For validation, several studies stated the comparison of their numerical results against experimental data, which generally involves comparing predicted heat transfer rates, thermal resistances, or other performance metrics with experimentally obtained values with excellent agreements.
3.
Referring to the analysed studies, different nanoparticle materials can present variable long-term stability under cyclic heating and cooling. For instance, Al2O3 and Fe2O3 are typically exhibited perfect thermal stability and resistance to oxidation, preserving their dispersion and performance over time. However, carbon-based nanoparticles such as MWCNTs and GNPs can face stability concerns as a result to its potential oxidation and agglomeration. Accordingly, it can be said that metal oxides can provide better long-term stability in nanofluids in a comparison to carbon-based materials.
4.
The measurement of thermal conductivity in NFs remains inconsistent across studies, as results vary significantly depending on the techniques and experimental conditions used. A standardized methodology for thermal characterization is urgently needed to ensure data comparability and reproducibility.
5.
The integration of NFs into HPs may necessitate a re-evaluation of traditional HP designs. Although several experimental and theoretical studies have reported enhanced performance, the specific design parameters that govern efficient NF-based HP operation remain unclear. Further investigations are required to understand the influence of geometry and operating conditions on performance optimisation.
6.
The synthesis and integration of nanoparticles into base fluids can be expensive, posing a significant barrier to commercial adoption. Economical methods for nanoparticle synthesis and stabilization, along with comprehensive cost–benefit analyses of NF-based systems, are essential for practical deployment in thermal applications.
7.
Most studies focus on short-term or steady-state performance, while the influence of NFs on long-term reliability and performance under varying operational conditions is largely unexplored. More research is needed on NF stability under thermal cycling, aging, and realistic usage scenarios.
8.
The environmental and health implications of NF use require careful evaluation. Given the potential toxicity and long-term ecological impact of nanoparticles, studies must be conducted to establish safe handling, disposal, and exposure guidelines to mitigate risks to both humans and the environment.
9.
One of the most critical challenges is the absence of standardized protocols or regulatory frameworks for the use of NFs in thermal systems. Since the application of NFs in HPs is relatively new, it is essential to develop clear guidelines and standards to ensure safe, efficient, and trustworthy deployment, which would also help accelerate market acceptance.
10.
The implication of Nanofluids in different types of heat pipes can be ascertained in different industrial applications. First, the refrigeration and waste heat recovery are examples of thermo-syphon heat pipes, which signify an improvement of thermal conductivity and efficient heat transfer due to incorporating NFs [81]. Second, electronic cooling systems are oscillating heat pipes that can have advantages of having NFs to enhance flow dynamics, besides assuring rapid heat dissipation. Third, incorporating NFs into screen mesh heat pipes can elucidate increased capillary action, making them perfect for portable electronic devices. Fourth, emerging NFs into wick-type heat pipes can assure optimum thermal performance in automotive cooling systems, which can lead to enhanced engine efficiency and reduced emissions.
11.
Talented surface treatment techniques were used to avoid sedimentation and agglomeration of nanoparticles in nanofluids. These include the functionalization, ultra-sonication, electrostatic and magnetic stabilization. These specifically used to improve the dispersion, coating with surfactants, break up agglomerates during preparation, increase surface charge and reduce clumping, and assuring a magnetic stabilization for magnetic nanoparticles using external fields. Overall, these methods can improve the stability and performance of nanofluids in different applications.

This review presented a comprehensive overview of the current state-of-the-art in the utilisation of NFs for enhancing heat transfer in various types of HPs. The application of NFs in heat transfer systems represents a significant advancement over conventional heat transfer fluids, with the potential to address many existed performance limitations. The key findings and implications of this review are summarised below:
1.
The experimental studies demonstrated that the addition of nanoparticles, such as Al2O3, CuO, and carbon nanotubes, to base fluids significantly improves thermal conductivity, thereby enhancing the heat transfer rate in HPs.
2.
The review indicated that a higher nanoparticle concentration is not always beneficial. Excessive loading can lead to increased thermal resistance and diminished performance.
3.
The critical nanoparticle concentration beyond which thermal resistance starts growing as a result to viscosity or sedimentation differs across various analysed studies. However, a common trend was perceived, where several studies indicated that thermal resistance tends to grow at greater nanoparticle concentrations, especially when concentrations surpass 2-5 vol.% for certain nanofluids. For example, Ghanbarpour et al. [62] and Herrera et al. [68] elucidated that concentrations above 5 % and 1 % respectively can lead to increased thermal resistance. Thus, it can be concluded that a critical concentration around 2-5 vol.% is often the threshold, beyond which opposing impacts such as increased viscosity and sedimentation begin to outweigh the advantages of improved thermal conductivity.
4.
Optimal thermal resistance is typically achieved with nanoparticle concentrations in the range between 0.1–0.3 wt.%. Deviations from this range often result in reduced heat transfer efficiency.
5.
Using ternary hybrid nanofluid of Al2O3, Cu, and TiO2 at 0.3 vol.% concentration in Thermo-syphon HP demonstrated the lowest thermal resistance of 0.09 K/W and the greatest heat transfer enhancement efficiency of 22.5 %.
6.
Using 0.009 % vol.% of silver nanoparticles in screen mesh HP significantly lowered heat resistance by 76.2 %, besides an improvement of 52.7 % in the evaporation heat transfer coefficient.
7.
Referring to wick HP, a 56 % increase in heat transfer was assured by using 4 % Cu-water nanofluid.
8.
Thermo-syphon, oscillating, screen mesh, and wick-type HPs exhibited variable degrees of performance improvement when charged with NFs. Each configuration benefits from specific enhancements, for example, increased capillary action in screen mesh HPs and improved flow dynamics in oscillating HPs.
9.
Modifications to HP geometry, such as the inclusion of convergent and divergent sections, have further improved performance when used in conjunction with NFs. These findings underscored the importance of geometric optimisation to achieve optimum thermal efficiency.
10.
Although short-term benefits of using NFs in HPs are well-documented, more research is needed on the long-term stability and effectiveness of NFs. Issues such as nanoparticle agglomeration and sedimentation remain major concerns affecting sustained performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Critical issues and recommendations for future studies
ce:section.ce:para:
To further advance the understanding and application of NFs in HP technology, the following key recommendations are proposed for future research:
1.
Future studies should examine a broader range of nanoparticles, including various composites and hybrid nanostructures, in conjunction with different HP materials. This may lead to the discovery of NFs with superior thermal conductivity and long-term stability.
2.
Limited research has addressed the long-term stability of NFs. More comprehensive studies are needed to assess the effects of temperature fluctuations, pressure changes, and environmental conditions on nanoparticle sedimentation and agglomeration. These insights will help evaluate the practical applicability of NFs in real-world thermal systems.
3.
Discrepancies in reported thermal properties are common due to inconsistent measurement methods. Future research should focus on establishing standardized protocols for quantifying thermal conductivity, viscosity, and other key thermo-physical properties to ensure reproducibility and comparability across studies.
4.
A number of mathematical models are recommended to successfully capture the thermal behaviour of nanofluids. These include the Einstein-Batchelor model that can predict the thermal conductivity for dilute nanofluids with spherical nanoparticles. Also, the Hamilton-Crosser model can be used for non-spherical particles and higher volume fractions. Also, the Maxwell-Garnett model is valuable to effectively estimate the thermal conductivity in composite materials. Finally, the Brinkman model can efficiently address the viscosity impacts in flow applications.
5.
Further work is needed to optimise HP geometries, including diameter, length, and internal wick structures, for effective NF utilisation. Computational fluid dynamics simulations can be employed to predict performance across variable operating conditions and guide experimental design.
6.
Economic studies should be conducted to evaluate the viability of implementing NFs in HP systems. This includes analysing the cost of nanoparticle synthesis, overall system integration, and potential energy savings. Such evaluations are essential for assessing the commercial feasibility of NF-based heat transfer technologies.
7.
Detailed studies are required to assess the environmental and health impacts of NFs. This includes evaluating nanoparticle toxicity, their behaviour and transport in ecosystems, and the development of guidelines for safe handling, disposal, and risk mitigation. A thorough understanding of these factors is vital for responsible and sustainable deployment.
8.
Future work should focus on tailoring NF–HP systems for specific applications, such as automotive cooling, renewable energy systems, and electronic device thermal management. Application-driven studies will support the development of targeted, efficient solutions across diverse industrial sectors with varying thermal requirements.
No funding was received.
